repo.name: secp256k1
repo.description: Optimized C library for EC operations on curve secp256k1

events.message: ci/cirrus: Bring back skeleton .cirrus.yml without jobs

events.body: cc @real-or-random Any thoughts on this or bike-shed ideas?

Concept ACK

There are a few lines added in the second commit and removed again in the third commit. I suggest just squashing the two commits.

This solves one item in #1392, cc @hebasto.

Where is the docker image cache located?

Is it possible to demonstrate the `build_script` with unpopulated cache?

> Where is the docker image cache located?

It is using the Bitcoin Core persistent workers.

> Is it possible to demonstrate the `build_script` with unpopulated cache?

Yes, you can just pick the task with the longest runtime: https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4555435512954880

> There are a few lines added in the second commit and removed again in the third commit. I suggest just squashing the two commits.

Who should be the author of the commit then?

> Who should be the author of the commit then?

You and add me in `Co-authored-by:`? Or the other way around, I don't care. Happy to flip a coin for you if you can't decide. :)

> You and add me in `Co-authored-by:`?

Thx, done

verification.payload:
tree 6a9ec3571fa343ec1e339f1e992477cd749271da
parent 2262d0eaab2aa1181f900acd2d8cd568047599b6
author MarcoFalke <*~=`'#}+{/-|&$^_@721217.xyz> 1695230027 +0000
committer MarcoFalke <*~=`'#}+{/-|&$^_@721217.xyz> 1695229794 +0000

ci/cirrus: Add native ARM64 persistent workers

Co-authored-by: Tim Ruffing <crypto@timruffing.de>

events.message:
ci/cirrus: Add native ARM64 persistent workers

Co-authored-by: Tim Ruffing <crypto@timruffing.de>

events.body:
> > You and add me in `Co-authored-by:`?
> 
> Thx, done

Now it's authored *and* co-authored by me. :D  

edit: Nevermind, I'm wrong. For some reason, github doesn't show your picture here, so I assumed it's just me.
re-ACK fa4d6c76b6dc249d02c926ad75725556614fdddd, only last two commits have been squashed since my recent [review](https://github.com/bitcoin-core/secp256k1/pull/1426#pullrequestreview-1636119941).

@MarcoFalke Follow-up question: Can the persistent runner run ARM32 binaries natively? From what I remember, most ARM64 machines can do this, but it depends on the CPU. If yes, we could add jobs tests for this, too.

Yes:

```
lscpu | grep op-mode
CPU op-mode(s):                     32-bit, 64-bit

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -29,11 +29,14 @@ RUN apt-get update && apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
         gcc-i686-linux-gnu libc6-dev-i386-cross libc6-dbg:i386 libubsan1:i386 libasan8:i386 \
         gcc-s390x-linux-gnu libc6-dev-s390x-cross libc6-dbg:s390x \
         gcc-arm-linux-gnueabihf libc6-dev-armhf-cross libc6-dbg:armhf \
-        gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu libc6-dev-arm64-cross libc6-dbg:arm64 \
         gcc-powerpc64le-linux-gnu libc6-dev-ppc64el-cross libc6-dbg:ppc64el \
         gcc-mingw-w64-x86-64-win32 wine64 wine \
         gcc-mingw-w64-i686-win32 wine32 \
-        python3
+        python3 && \
+        if ! ( dpkg --print-architecture | grep --quiet "arm64" ) ; then \
+         apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
+         gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu libc6-dev-arm64-cross libc6-dbg:arm64 ;\
+        fi
comments.body:
Strictly speaking, this is unrelated to this PR, but I think it's good practice/idiom to clean the cache after every `apt-get` to make the cached layer smaller, so we should add it here

```suggestion
        fi && \
        apt-get clean && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -29,11 +29,14 @@ RUN apt-get update && apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
         gcc-i686-linux-gnu libc6-dev-i386-cross libc6-dbg:i386 libubsan1:i386 libasan8:i386 \
         gcc-s390x-linux-gnu libc6-dev-s390x-cross libc6-dbg:s390x \

-        python3
+        python3 && \

comments.body: On an `arm64` worker, other cross-toolchains are also not used. Maybe move them into this `if` block?

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -29,11 +29,14 @@ RUN apt-get update && apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
         gcc-i686-linux-gnu libc6-dev-i386-cross libc6-dbg:i386 libubsan1:i386 libasan8:i386 \
         gcc-s390x-linux-gnu libc6-dev-s390x-cross libc6-dbg:s390x \
         gcc-arm-linux-gnueabihf libc6-dev-armhf-cross libc6-dbg:armhf \
-        gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu libc6-dev-arm64-cross libc6-dbg:arm64 \
         gcc-powerpc64le-linux-gnu libc6-dev-ppc64el-cross libc6-dbg:ppc64el \
         gcc-mingw-w64-x86-64-win32 wine64 wine \
         gcc-mingw-w64-i686-win32 wine32 \
-        python3
+        python3 && \
+        if ! ( dpkg --print-architecture | grep --quiet "arm64" ) ; then \
+         apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y \
+         gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu libc6-dev-arm64-cross libc6-dbg:arm64 ;\
comments.body: The reason to keep these packages is that the Docker image can then be used by developers locally and has all the tools available for testing (if they have ARM machines, but this is not unlikely with recent Mac hardware). And okay, yeah, it costs a bit of time on CI, but I assume it's in the range of seconds?!

         gcc-i686-linux-gnu libc6-dev-i386-cross libc6-dbg:i386 libubsan1:i386 libasan8:i386 \

+        python3 && \

comments.body: It is actually a presumed upstream bug that this list of install needs to be touched. It should not result in a bug to install a package.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,95 @@
+env:
+  ### cirrus config
+  CIRRUS_CLONE_DEPTH: 1
+  ### compiler options
+  HOST:
+  WRAPPER_CMD:
+  # Specific warnings can be disabled with -Wno-error=foo.
+  # -pedantic-errors is not equivalent to -Werror=pedantic and thus not implied by -Werror according to the GCC manual.
+  WERROR_CFLAGS: -Werror -pedantic-errors
+  MAKEFLAGS: -j4
+  BUILD: check
+  ### secp256k1 config
+  ECMULTWINDOW: auto
+  ECMULTGENPRECISION: auto
+  ASM: no
+  WIDEMUL: auto
+  WITH_VALGRIND: yes
+  EXTRAFLAGS:
+  ### secp256k1 modules
+  EXPERIMENTAL: no
+  ECDH: no
+  RECOVERY: no
+  SCHNORRSIG: no
+  ELLSWIFT: no
+  ### test options
+  SECP256K1_TEST_ITERS:
+  BENCH: yes
+  SECP256K1_BENCH_ITERS: 2
+  CTIMETESTS: yes
+  # Compile and run the tests
+  EXAMPLES: yes
+
+cat_logs_snippet: &CAT_LOGS
+  always:
+    cat_tests_log_script:
+      - cat tests.log || true
+    cat_noverify_tests_log_script:
+      - cat noverify_tests.log || true
+    cat_exhaustive_tests_log_script:
+      - cat exhaustive_tests.log || true
+    cat_ctime_tests_log_script:
+      - cat ctime_tests.log || true
+    cat_bench_log_script:
+      - cat bench.log || true
+    cat_config_log_script:
+      - cat config.log || true
+    cat_test_env_script:
+      - cat test_env.log || true
+    cat_ci_env_script:
+      - env
+
+linux_arm64_container_snippet: &LINUX_ARM64_CONTAINER
+  env_script:
+    - env | tee /tmp/env
+  build_script:
+    - DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --file "ci/linux-debian.Dockerfile" --tag="ci_secp256k1_arm"
+    - docker image prune --force  # Cleanup stale layers
+  test_script:
+    - docker run --rm --mount "type=bind,src=./,dst=/ci_secp256k1" --env-file /tmp/env --replace --name "ci_secp256k1_arm" "ci_secp256k1_arm" bash -c "cd /ci_secp256k1/ && ./ci/ci.sh"
comments.body:
@maflcko  
I think `--replace` is a podman-only argument. `docker run` doesn't seem to have it. Should we just rewrite the command to use the "native" podman syntax? I guess there's no point in having an invalid docker command here.

Yes, if you prefer to call `podman` here, you can do that.

Both commands will redirect to `podman`, currently.

On a second thought, when using `podman`, this should probably specify `--external`, because build images will be considered external.

(No rush to change anything here, though. The hosts will occasionally clean up external images already)
